one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning GPT-4o-mini turns a cheap commodity LLM into a state-of-the-art educational-PII detector that beats Presidio and Azure on recall, roughly triples their precision, costs ~5x less than Azure per token, and closes their African/Asian name-detection fairness gap, with an optional verifier stage to trade recall for higher precision.
key_finding: On CRAPII, fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini gives the best overall recall (0.9589) versus Azure (0.9212) and Presidio-trf (0.8368), while roughly tripling precision over the recall-oriented baselines (0.6042 vs Azure 0.2462, Presidio-trf 0.2092) and achieving the top F5 (0.9377) at about $0.92 per 1M tokens vs Azure's $4.90. Adding the verifier lifts precision to the highest of all methods (0.8893 no-CoT, 0.7600 CoT) at the cost of recall (0.8023 / 0.8648), exposing the precision-recall trade-off. Fairness: the fine-tuned model shows no statistically significant recall gap across gender or culture (African 0.9748, Asian 0.9840, European 0.9756, American 0.9790), whereas Presidio and Azure significantly under-detect African/Asian names (Presidio African recall 0.7647, Asian 0.8640). Generalizability on TSCC: fine-tuning the CRAPII-tuned model on only 10 TSCC transcripts reaches Precision 0.9708 / Recall 0.9895 / F1 0.9801, and the CRAPII-tuned model prompted zero-shot on TSCC reaches near-perfect precision 0.9984 (only 2 false positives) but lower recall 0.7882.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is an empirical study showing that a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini can serve as an accurate, low-cost PII detector for educational text, plus three add-ons the authors build themselves: (1) a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini NER model that marks PII with category-specific special tokens (e.g. @@@name###, QQQemail^^^) and extracts spans by regex to sidestep GPT positional-hallucination; (2) a two-step 'verifier' model (also a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini, in a no-CoT and a CoT variant) that re-checks each detected entity in context to prune false positives and raise precision; (3) a cultural/gender fairness analysis and (4) a cross-dataset generalizability (domain-adaptation) test. NOTE for graph integrity: the Hidden-in-Plain-Sight (HIPS) surrogate-substitution method, BRATsynthetic, GPT-NER, and the Zambrano et al. 2025 benchmark are CITED prior work, not this paper's inventions. This paper deliberately scopes itself to the detection/NER stage that HIPS depends on, and does NOT itself implement or evaluate an end-to-end HIPS anonymization pipeline.
threat_model: Data-privacy / re-identification threat, not an adversarial-attack defense. The asset is Personally Identifiable Information (student/teacher names, emails, personal URLs, phone numbers, IDs, addresses) embedded in educational corpora (MOOC essays, tutoring chat logs) that researchers want to share/analyze. The risk is that imperfect de-identification leaks PII (false negatives -> privacy breach, GDPR/FERPA violation) while over-redaction (false positives) destroys dataset utility. The paper argues both matter: missed PII exposes individuals, and low precision under a HIPS surrogate-replacement scheme corrupts non-PII content (e.g. replacing 'Newton method' or 'Nazareth'). The Discussion also touches on deployment-side risks it does not experimentally test: sending data to OpenAI's API (prompt-injection / training-data extraction, citing Nasr et al. 2025) and malicious code in downloaded open-source models (reverse-shell attacks). These are contextual caveats, not evaluated attacks.
limitations: Evaluation covers only 'easy', commonly-supported PII types (names, emails, URLs, phones) and explicitly excludes harder/indirect identifiers (addresses, IDs, dates, ages) that need deeper context. PHONE_NUM statistics are near-meaningless: only 15 phone entities exist in all of CRAPII (9 in test), so per-category phone metrics are extremely noisy. Even the best detector's raw precision is only moderate (0.6042), so real deployments need the verifier stage, which then sacrifices recall. Only two commercial/rule-based baselines (Presidio, Azure) are compared; no open-source LLM baseline (e.g. LLaMA) is actually run here. The generalizability test uses a single second dataset (TSCC) with synthetically re-inserted names, not naturally occurring data. Deployment-security concerns (API prompt injection, malicious open-source model code) are discussed but not experimentally evaluated. The end-to-end HIPS anonymization benefit is argued qualitatively via three examples, not measured.

datasets_used: CRAPII (Cleaned Repository of Annotated PII) - 22,688 MOOC student-writing docs, 4,871 labeled PII words; 7 annotated categories but study uses 4 (NAME_STUDENT, URL_PERSONAL, EMAIL, PHONE_NUM); split 25% Base Train / 15% Verifier Train / 60% Test
TSCC (Teacher-Student Chatroom Corpus) - 260 tutoring sessions, ~41.4K conversational turns, ~362K word tokens; used for generalizability with synthetic culture/gender-balanced names
quantitative_results: Fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.6042, Recall 0.9589, F1 0.7413, F5 0.9377
Prompting GPT-4o-mini (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.6593, Recall 0.7781, F1 0.7138, F5 0.7727
Azure AI Language (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.2462, Recall 0.9212, F1 0.3885, F5 0.8333
Presidio en_core_web_trf (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.2092, Recall 0.8368; Presidio en_core_web_lg: Precision 0.1505, Recall 0.7103
Verifier Model I no-CoT (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.8893, Recall 0.8023, F1 0.8435 (highest precision of all methods)
Verifier Model II with-CoT (CRAPII, overall): Precision 0.7600, Recall 0.8648, F1 0.8091
Cost per 1M tokens: fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini $0.92 (total $11.93) vs Azure AI Language $4.90 (total $63.27) vs prompting GPT-4o-mini $0.40; Presidio $0

Fairness (recall by culture, fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini): Europe 0.9756, Americas 0.9790, Asia 0.9840, Africa 0.9748 - no significant Mann-Whitney U difference; baselines significantly worse on African names (Presidio 0.7647, Azure 0.9244) and Asian names (Presidio 0.8640, Azure 0.9320 p<0.01)

TSCC generalizability: fine-tuned-GPT-4o-mini + further fine-tuning on 10 transcripts = Precision 0.9708, Recall 0.9895, F1 0.9801, F5 0.9887; CRAPII-fine-tuned model zero-shot on TSCC = Precision 0.9984 (2 FPs), Recall 0.7882; few-shot prompted GPT-4o-mini on TSCC = Recall 0.9932, Precision 0.7145
baselines_compared: Microsoft Presidio with spaCy en_core_web_lg (CNN, 382 MB)
Microsoft Presidio with spaCy en_core_web_trf (transformer, 436 MB)
Few-shot-prompted GPT-4o-mini (the paper's own prompting variant, also serving as a comparison point to the fine-tuned model)
